title: Please write essay of 2000 words to explore how Samsung executes various price and non-price strategies in an attempt to…

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Please write essay of 2000 words to explore how Samsung executes various price and non-price strategies in an attempt to establish and maintain a competitive advantage in the competition with Apple. In so doing, illustrate the inter-dependencies via an action-reaction sequence of events. 
Your essay should critically 1. assess the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools. 
2. outline the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts introduced in this Module. 
3. illustrate how the transaction cost economics and the economics of game theory can be used to reshape strategy. 
The best way to approach the assignment is to focus on concerns may have to do with (i) market entry (ii) product launch (iii) challenges of technology or (iv) prices. So, 1. proceed to analyse the market-asa-game, 2. identify and filter the competitive threat, 3. assess the action-reaction sequence of events and 4. evaluate your results. And always 5. provide a risk assessment of the likely competitive threats facing a company on choice of strategy.

Please remember to make citation from the following articles:

Akshay R., R. (2000). How to Fight a Price War. Harvard Business Review,.
Bill, G. (2014). How to Outsmart Activist Investors. Harvard Business Review,.
Dan, A. (2009). The End of Rational Economics. Harvard Business Review, 87(7).
Feng, Z. (2019). Why some platforms thrive… and others don’t . Harvard business review,.
Hanna, H. (2014). THE LIMITS OF SCALE. Harvard Business Review,.
Kevin P., C. (2009). Predicting Your Competitor’s Reaction. Harvard Business Review,.
McNutt , P. (n.d.). Decoding Strategy: Patterns and Predictions, Second Edition.
McNutt, P. (n.d.). Economics of Strategy.
Tang, C.S. (2010). A review of marketing–operations interface models: From co-existence to coordination and collaboration. International Journal of Production Economics, 125(1), pp.22–40. doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpe.2010.01.014.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Introduction

Samsung and Apple have been competing in the smartphone and electronic devices industry for several years. Both companies have established a strong brand name and have a significant market share. However, Samsung has been able to gain a competitive advantage over Apple by implementing various price and non-price strategies. This essay will explore how Samsung executes these strategies, including market entry, product launch, challenges of technology, and prices, to establish and maintain a competitive advantage in the competition with Apple. Additionally, the essay will illustrate the inter-dependencies via an action-reaction sequence of events, critically assess the value-added of transaction cost economics and game theory tools, and outline the overlap between micro-economics and game theoretic concepts introduced in this Module.

Market-asa-Game Analysis

According to McNutt (n.d.), the market can be perceived as a game, where each company is a player who is trying to gain a competitive advantage over the other players. One of the key concepts of game theory is that the players are interdependent, meaning that the actions of one player can affect the outcome for all players. This interdependence is particularly relevant in the case of Samsung and Apple, as the actions of one company, such as a new product launch or price change, can significantly affect the market share and profits of the other company.

Identifying and Filtering the Competitive Threat

To gain a competitive advantage over Apple, Samsung must continually analyze the market and identify potential competitive threats. One of the strategies that Samsung has used is to offer a wider variety of products at different price points. For example, Samsung offers smartphones at various price points, from low-end to high-end, to appeal to a wider range of customers. This strategy can help Samsung to capture market share from Apple by appealing to customers who may not be able to afford Apple's high-end products.

Assessing the Action-Reaction Sequence of Events

Before implementing a new strategy, Samsung must assess the potential reaction of Apple, as this reaction could significantly affect the success of the new strategy. For example, if Samsung were to launch a new high-end smartphone, it is likely that Apple would respond with its own new high-end smartphone. To prepare for this potential reaction, Samsung must ensure that its new smartphone offers unique features or advantages that make it more appealing than Apple's new smartphone.

Value-Added of Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory Tools

Transaction cost economics and game theory tools can help Samsung to gain a competitive advantage by providing a framework for analyzing the market and making strategic decisions. Transaction cost economics can help Samsung to assess the costs and benefits of various transactional activities, such as market entry, product launch, and pricing decisions. Game theory tools can help Samsung to analyze the interdependent nature of the market and assess the likely reactions of Apple to new strategies.

Overlap between Micro-economics and Game Theoretic Concepts

Micro-economics and game theoretic concepts are closely related, as micro-economic concepts are often used as the basis for game theory models. For example, the concept of supply and demand is a fundamental micro-economic concept that is often used in game theory models to analyze market behavior. Additionally, game theory models often use concepts such as Nash equilibrium and dominant strategy, which are closely related to micro-economic concepts of cost-benefit analysis and optimization.

Reshaping Strategy with Transaction Cost Economics and the Economics of Game Theory

Transaction cost economics and the economics of game theory can be used to reshape Samsung's strategy by providing a framework for analyzing the costs and benefits of different strategies and assessing the likely reactions of Apple. For example, transaction cost economics can be used to analyze the costs and benefits of entering a new market, while game theory can be used to assess the likely competitive response of Apple to a new product launch. By using these tools, Samsung can make more informed strategic decisions and gain a competitive advantage over Apple.

Risk Assessment of Likely Competitive Threats

One of the key risks facing Samsung is the potential for Apple to respond aggressively to new strategies. For example, if Samsung were to launch a new product that is significantly better than Apple's products, Apple could respond by lowering its prices or launching its own new product that offers similar features. Additionally, Samsung must be aware of the potential for negative reactions from customers or regulatory bodies, which could significantly affect its market share and profits.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Samsung has been able to gain a competitive advantage over Apple by implementing various price and non-price strategies, including offering a wide variety of products at different price points and launching new products with unique features. To continue to gain a competitive advantage, Samsung must continually analyze the market and identify potential competitive threats. Additionally, transaction cost economics and game theory tools can help Samsung to make more informed strategic decisions by analyzing the costs and benefits of different strategies and assessing the likely competitive response of Apple. Ultimately, Samsung must be aware of the risks associated with new strategies and develop strategies that mitigate these risks while capitalizing on opportunities to gain a competitive advantage.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Introduction

Samsung and Apple are two of the world's most recognizable brands in the electronic devices’ industry. The competition between them has been fierce as they constantly launch new products with unique features and innovative designs. To gain a competitive advantage over Apple, Samsung has implemented various strategies, including both price and non-price techniques. In this essay, we will explore how Samsung has executed these strategies to establish and maintain its competitive advantage in the highly competitive smartphone market. Specifically, we will focus on market entry, product launch, challenges of technology, and pricing.

Market-as-a-Game Analysis

The market can be viewed as a game in which each player must strategically make moves to gain a competitive advantage. In the smartphone market, Samsung and Apple are interdependent players that influence each other's profits and market share. Thus, any move made by Samsung can affect Apple's response and vice versa. To succeed in this game, Samsung must understand how to predict Apple's reaction and respond appropriately, considering the potential long-term consequences of any decision.

Identifying and Filtering the Competitive Threat

To compete with Apple, Samsung must constantly analyze the market and identify potential competitive threats. One effective strategy Samsung has used is to diversify its portfolio and offer a wide range of products at different price points, colors, features, and sizes. This strategy caters for the different tastes and budgets of its customers and has helped Samsung capture market share from Apple and other competitors. In comparison, Apple primarily targets the high-end market with its premium products and price points, leaving some potential customers out of its reach.

Assessing the Action-Reaction Sequence of Events

Samsung must anticipate Apple's reaction and respond strategically to gain a competitive advantage. For example, if Samsung were to launch a new product, it should have unique features that Apple does not have or cannot easily replicate. If Apple releases a new device, Samsung should evaluate its qualities and try to incorporate similar yet distinctive capabilities into its products, giving consumers an added reason to choose its products over Apple's. Samsung can also use non-price policies, such as product design, packaging, or promotion, to differentiate itself from Apple in the market.

Value-Added of Transaction Cost Economics and Game Theory Tools

Transaction cost economics and game theory provide a framework for analyzing the market and making strategic decisions. They can help Samsung gain a competitive advantage, offering a perspective on costs and benefits when launching new products, pricing strategies, and entering new markets. Game theory specifically analyzes the interdependence of players in the market and the possible responses of competitors, providing critical insights into the dynamics of the market.

Overlap between Microeconomics and Game Theoretic Concepts

Microeconomic concepts such as supply and demand, market structure analysis, and game theory concepts, have a strong link. Game theory models commonly use microeconomic principles to analyze market behavior, player strategies, and interactions and outcomes. Concepts like Nash equilibrium, dominant strategy, and mixed strategies are closely related to microeconomics concepts of optimization and cost-benefit analysis.

Reshaping Strategy with Transaction Cost Economics and the Economics of Game Theory

Transaction cost economics and the economics of game theory can aid Samsung in making more informed decisions that drive nimble changes in its strategies while analyzing the costs and benefits of different approaches. When devising pricing and promotion strategies, transaction cost economics can aid Samsung in calculating costs like advertising or distribution expenses, while game theory can provide a perspective on how its competitor(s) might react. Therefore, Samsung can use these principles to refine its strategy and gain a competitive edge.

Market Entry Strategies

For Samsung, entering new markets requires assessing the nature of the market, the target audiences, and competitors. The company has utilized divergent strategic approaches to enter new markets. For example, when Samsung entered the U.S market, it partnered with retail chain Best Buy to provide a retail space to sell its products. Samsung offered value-added benefits, such as high-end customer service to differentiate itself from its competitor. Additionally, Samsung has formed partnerships in emerging markets, working with carriers and content providers to add value to its products in those markets.

Product Launch Strategies

Samsung's product launch strategy involves releasing products that are also distinct from Apple's while building upon the strengths of its offerings. When Samsung launched the Galaxy S6 in 2015, it relied on a comprehensive market research to determine what features and specifications were necessary to outdo Apple’s flagship product at the time, the iPhone 6. Samsung amplified its differentiation strategy with the introduction of features such as the camera system and a water-resistant experience.

Challenges of Technology

Technology is a key factor in the competition between Samsung and Apple. Therefore, both companies actively review developments in technology to ensure they keep pace with modern trends. For example, Samsung is now developing foldable screen technology, giving it an opportunity to corner another market niche that Apple has yet to explore.

Pricing Strategies

Price is a key determinant in consumer decision-making. Samsung's pricing strategy tends to be related to its competitive posture vis-à-vis Apple. Samsung has launched product offensives in instances where it believes it offers exceptionally competitive prices, aiming to gain a higher market share when Apple’s products would be considered relatively high end. In a counter-offensive, Samsung also adjusts its prices in response to Apple's pricing strategies to maintain a viable profit margin even as it competes with the dominant player.

Assessing Competitive Threats

Samsung faces challenges from competitors that aim to take a segment of its market share, particularly Apple. Additionally, as new firms enter the market, competition increases, posing a potential threat to Samsung's market share. Product innovation by new competitors can be a severe competitive threat, pushing Samsung to innovate further and provide consumers with distinct choices that a new entrant cannot replicate easily. 

Conclusion

Samsung's strategies have given it a competitive advantage over Apple in popular markets worldwide. Transaction cost economics and game theory have provided the tools for Samsung to develop and implement these strategies successfully. Samsung has capitalized on diversification by offering a wide range of products designed to appeal to different demographics, prices, and tastes. Samsung also uses product launches, innovation, pricing, and entering new markets as strategies to maintain its competitive advantage against Apple. In conclusion, Samsung must keep on reevaluating and adapting its strategies to surmount the competitive threats it faces, as the game dynamic requires constant vigilance and adaptation.
